title: Navajo plying (chain plying) from a single cop
subtitle: Making a balanced 3-ply yarn from a single cop using the chain-ply loop method
excerpt: Navajo plying (also called chain plying) produces a 3-ply yarn from a single cop of singles by chaining loops through each other as the singles ply. The technique preserves colour sequences in variegated yarn, produces a balanced 3-ply without a second cop, and is useful for small quantities where spinning two matching cops is impractical. The finished yarn is identical in structure to a conventional 3-ply.
sourceNotes: Chain plying (often called Navajo plying in contemporary craft contexts) is a widely documented technique in spinning instruction. The name is a misnomer: it is not a traditional Navajo technique. Technique is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A plying technique in which loops of singles are chained through each other (like a crochet chain) while being plied. The result is a 3-ply structure from a single cop.

Yarn in which the twist of the ply exactly offsets the twist of the singles, so the finished skein hangs straight rather than coiling. Testing for balance: hang a skein loop from a single finger — a balanced yarn hangs without twisting.

content.text: is worked at the spinning wheel. The singles leave the bobbin and are drawn through a series of loops chained through each other by the non-dominant hand. The three strands of the chain are plied onto an empty bobbin with an S-twist (opposite the Z-twist of the singles). The result is a

items.name: Full bobbin of Z-twist singles on the wheel
Lazy kate or second bobbin holder (for feeding the singles)

content.text: Place the full singles bobbin on the lazy kate. Thread the yarn through the wheel's flyer hooks and attach to the empty bobbin. Set the wheel to ply (S-twist: treadle in the opposite direction from spinning, or use a counter-ply wheel setting).
Step 2: Form the first chain loop

Begin with the yarn attached to the leader on the bobbin. Form a loop about 30 cm long in the singles by reaching through the strand and pulling a bight of yarn forward. The three strands forming the chain are: the two sides of this loop, and the incoming singles feeding from the lazy kate.

Hold the three strands together between the fingers of the non-dominant hand and allow the wheel to ply them together with S-twist. Let the yarn feed through your fingers at a steady rate. As the chain plies toward the knotted end of each loop, use the dominant hand to reach through the current loop and pull the next bight of singles forward to form a new loop.

After 2 m of chain ply, stop and hold a loop of the plied yarn on one finger. A balanced yarn hangs straight. Coiling one direction means under-plied; coiling the other means over-plied. Adjust the number of treadle strokes per unit length to correct.
Step 5: Wind off and set the twist

When the bobbin is full or the singles are used, wind the plied yarn off onto the niddy-noddy into a hank. Tie loosely at two points. Soak in lukewarm water for 10 minutes, then snap the skein firmly between both hands several times. Hang to dry with a small weight at the bottom to set the twist.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems with chain plying.

items.symptom: Knot at loop junction shows in the finished yarn
items.cause: Loop transitions tightened too quickly before plying could absorb the join.
items.fix: Allow a slightly longer overlap at each loop transition (3 to 4 cm rather than 1 to 2 cm) and ply through the transition slowly to let the twist smooth the join.

items.symptom: Ply keeps snapping at loop junctions
items.cause: Singles are weak or over-twisted at the join.
items.fix: The junction of two loops has six strands momentarily rather than three; ply through this point more slowly and with less take-up tension.
